rulings.Case Title: JUNE AURE VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Deft. Baggage Systems, Inc.'S Motion For Leave To File Its Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 17, 2015, Line 9, Defendant Baggage Systems, Inc.'s Motion For Leave To File Its Cross-Complaint. The motion is granted. The court liberally allows the filing of cross-complaints and the parties' respective liabilities should be determined in one action. BAE has been diligent and allowing the cross-complaint will not unduly prejudice any party. The court will not adjudicate the merits of the indemnity claim in this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: GRANVILLE MCCOLLOUGH VS. SAN FRANCISCO COUNTY SHERIFF'S DEPARTMENT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Defendants
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 17, 2015, Line 10, DEFENDANT SHERIFF ROSS MIRKARIMI, CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Defendants. Motion for summary judgment denied. Motion for summary adjudication granted in part and denied in part. As to the 1st cause of action ("COA") for FEHA Discrimination, the motion is denied. Plaintiff raises a triable issue of material fact as to whether he suffered an adverse employment action. There is evidence that Plaintiff was given excessive workload and duties compared to female sergeants and that Plaintiff was assigned unnecessary, menial tasks. (Plaintiff's Additional Material Facts ("AMF") 44, 48, 50.) Liberally construing the evidence in favor of Plaintiff, a reasonable jury could find that this materially affected the terms of Plaintiff's employment. Defendant has not set forth a legitimate, nondiscriminatory reason for Plaintiff being given an excessive workload and being assigned menial tasks. As to the 2nd COA for FEHA Retaliation, the motion is denied. As set forth above, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiff suffered an adverse employment action. P submits sufficient evidence to raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether there is a causal link between the adverse employment actions and the filing of complaints and whether his supervisors knew about the complaints. (AMF 48, 50, 54.) As to the 3rd COA for Labor Code Retaliation, the motion is denied. There is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiff complained about violations of federal or state statutes, such as FEHA discrimination and harassment. Mueller v. County of Los Angeles (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 809 and Carter v. Escondido Union High School Dist. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 922, cited by Defendant, are distinguishable. As to the 4th COA for harassment, Plaintiff has submitted sufficient evidence to raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether the conduct was severe and pervasive and that the conduct went beyond personnel management decisions. (AMF 14, 44, 47, 48, 68, 77, 90.) As to the 5th COA for IIED, the motion is denied. Plaintiff provides sufficient evidence to raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether the conduct went beyond "mere insults, indignities, threats, annoyances, petty oppressions, or other trivialities." (Fisher v. San Pedro Peninsula Hospital (1989) 214 Cal.App.3d 590, 617.) Further, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiff actually suffered emotional distress. (AMF 34.) As to the 6th COA for negligent supervision, the motion is granted. This cause of action is not based on a statute and is therefore barred as a matter of law. C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, cited by Plaintiff, is distinguishable. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: CHUCK AMITAL, SUCCESSOR TRUSTEE OF THE FRED S. VS. NORMANDY APARTMENTS, A CALIFORNIA GENERAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted [Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2033-280]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Tuesday, MArch 17, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF CHUCK AMITAL's Motion For Order To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition filed; motion granted in part. The Court notes that subsequent to the filing of this motion, Defendant provided a document that contained only objections and no substantive responses. However, all objections have been waived by the failure to serve timely responses. Defendants are ordered to provide supplemental responses to the RFA's by March 27, 2015. The responses shall either admit, deny, or state that Defendant has insufficient facts to admit or deny the RFA's. No qualified responses or any other verbiage shall be included. The motion to deem the requests admitted is denied without prejudice to re-filing if Defendant fails to comply with this Order. Sanctions of $1,000 are awarded to Plaintiff. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BERNADETTE TANGUILIG VS. BLOOMINGDALE'S, INC., AN OHIO CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 17, 2015, Line 8, DEFENDANT BLOOMINGDALE'S, INC., Motion To Compel Arbitration. The court denies the motion to compel arbitration. Plaintiff filed a representative PAGA action. (Complaint, par. 3). The pre-dispute waiver of plaintiff's ability to maintain such representative claims is unenforceable. In Iskanian v. CLS Transportation Los Angles, LLC (2014) 59 Cal.4th 348, 383, the court provides that: [A]n agreement by employees to waive their right to bring a PAGA action serves to disable one of the primary mechanisms for enforcing the Labor Code. Because such an agreement has as its "object, ... indirectly, to exempt [the employer] from responsibility for [its] own ... violation of law," it is against public policy and may not be enforced. (Civ.Code, § 1668.). Such an agreement also violates Civil Code section 3513 's injunction that "a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement." The PAGA was clearly established for a public reason, and agreements requiring the waiver of PAGA rights would harm the state's interests in enforcing the Labor Code and in receiving the proceeds of civil penalties used to deter violations. Of course, employees are free to choose whether or not to bring PAGA actions when they are aware of Labor Code violations. (See Armendariz, supra, 24 Cal.4th at p. 103, fn. 8, 99 Cal.Rptr.2d 745, 6 P.3d 669 [waivers freely made after a dispute has arisen are not necessarily contrary to public policy].) But it is contrary to public policy for an employment agreement to eliminate this choice altogether by requiring employees to waive the right to bring a PAGA action before any dispute arises. Iskanian is binding authority that this court must follow. The existence of the opt out procedure does not change the analysis and Securitas Security Services USA, Inc. v. Sup. Crt. (2015 WL 848954) does not compel a different result. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: JANET MILLER VS. ANNE M DISNEY FAMILY TRUST BY JAMES H DISNEY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash [Or Modify] Plaintiffs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To State Farm
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Tuesday, March 17, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT ANNE M DISNEY FAMILY TRUST BY JAMES H DISNEY Motion To Quash [Or Modify] Plaintiffs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To State Farm. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to quash granted without prejudice to the subpoena being re-served. The Court finds that the proof of service of the subpoena on State Farm was not served on Defendant within 10 days of the date of the deposition as required by CCP 1985.3(b), et. seq. If the subpoena is re-served, the term "premises" must be properly defined. The Court finds that neither party facilitated communication with the other on this issue, and therefore, denies the various requests for sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
